content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Cornmission
February 
■5′  ■99■
Linda coldberg
649-8 Woodside Si.erra
Sacramento, cA 95425
Re: Your Request for Advj-ce
Our F il,e No, A-91-065
Dear I'ls . Goldberg:
This tetter is in response to your reguest for advice and
confirms the advice provided to you in the course of our telephone
conversation of January 31, 1991, regarding your duti-es and
respons ibil ities under the provisions of the Polj-tical Reform Act
(thl "Actu).1
OUESTION
Under the provisions of the Act, may you accept discounted or
cornplimentary accommodations from the hotel industry?
CONCLUSION
Under the facts presented, the Act does not prohibit you frorn
accepting discounted or complirnentary accommodations from the
hotel industry.
FACTS
You are a designated enployee under the conflict-of-interest
code of the Assembly Rules committee. From time to tirne you have
subrnitted inforrnation regarding hotels, I'bed and breakfastrl
establishments and inns throughout the United States and canada to
Sandra Soule. Ms. soule is the publisher of a travel guide
entitled America's Wonderful Little Hotels and Tnns. You have
received no compensation for your contributions to this travel
guide.
1' 
covernment code sections 81000-91015. Al-1 statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Cornmission
regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations section
18ooo, et seq. Alt references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California code of Regul-ations.
428 J Street,Suite 800 ● P.0.Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804‐ 0807 ● (916)322´5660


File No. A-9l--o 65
Page No. 2
2 Th" tern rtstate agencyrr includes the Legislature' 
(section
82049. )
You wish to continue contributing to this publication- 
You
will receive no payments for your contributions. 
However, from
time to tj-me you may receive reimbursement for your expenses to
purchase and develop f ilrn in connection with your activities. 
You
iray also receive courtesy or discounted accommodations at certain
establishments that you wish to visit. 
You seek our advice to
deterrnj.ne whether you may accept these courtesy or discounted
accommodations in California and outside of california in the
future .
ANALYSIS
As amended effective January L, lg9l, the Act imposes certain
restrictions on payments received by designated employees of state
agencies.2 whereas receipt of incorne from the pursuit of a bona
fide business, trade, or profession is not restricted, the Act
prohibits receipt of honoraria and limits gifts to $250 in each
-calendar year from each single source. (Sections 89502 (c) and
8e5o5 (a) .
with respect to honoraria, Section 89503 states that:
(a) No menber of a state board or commission,
and no designated employee of a state agency, shall
accept an honorarium from any source, if the member
or eirployee would be required to report the receipt
of incone or gifts from that source on his or her
statement of economic interest. 
this section shall
not apply to any part-tiure member of the governing
board of any public institution of higher
education, unless the member is also an elected
official.
section 89503 (a) .
You have advised ne that you would be required to report
receipt of income or gifts fron providers of lodging under the
provi'sions of the coni l ict-of-interest 
code of the Assembly Rules
'Comrnittee. Therefore, if the discounted accornmodations constitute
an honorarium, the prohibitions of section 89503 (a) would be
applicable to Your facts.
The Act defines an honorarium as follo\'/s:
(b) rrHonorariurnrr means, except as provided in
subdivision (c), any palment made in consideration
for any speech given, article published, or
attendance at any public or private conference,


Fi■e No. A-9■-065
Page No. 3
convention, meeting, social event, neal, or like
gathering .
(c) 
trHonorarium'r does not include earned
income for personal services which are customarily
provided j-n connection with the individual,s
business or practice of a bona fide business,
trade, or profession, such as teaching, practicing
Iaw, nledicine, insurance, real estate, banking, or
building contracting, unless the sole or
predorninant activity of the business, trade, or
profession is making speeches. The commission
shal-I adopt regulations to implement this
subdivision.
Section 89502 (b) and (c).
When you arrive at your destination, you wil-I not be nakj.ng a
speech or attending a gathering. You will sinply take photographs
and observe the premises with a vj-ew to reporting your findings to
the editor of a travel guide. You are not receiving discounted
accomrnodat j,ons in consideration of an article prepared on behalf
of the j.nnkeepers. Rather you will subnit a report and
photographs to the editor of a travel guide who vri11 then include
the information as one of nany items related to lodging in
California. 
Therefore, the courtesy or discounted accommodations
you nay receive are not honoraria for purposes of the Act.
The courtesy or discounted accommodations are not j-ncorne from
a bona fide business, trade, or profession. You have advised me
that you do not treat your expenditures in connection with your
travel as a business deductj-on for tax purposes, you do not
receive compensation for your $rork, and in general you do not
perceive your activities 
as a business. You travel periodically
and share your findings with parties who may have an interest in
the information you roake available to then. However, e/hen you
receive courtesy or discounted accommodations, you have received a
payment which is reportable under the provisions of the Act.
Any courtesy or discounted accommodations you may receive are
gifts. 
Gift rneans:
... any paynent to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not
received and i,ncludes a rebate or discount in the
price of anything of value unless the rebate or
iiscount is nade in the reguJ.ar course of business
to members of the public without regard to official
status....
Section 82028 (a)
You will receive a discount in the price of the
accommodations. However, the provider of these accommodations


Fi■e NO. A-9■-065
Page No. 4
will not receive consideration from you in exchange for the
courtesy or discounted accommodations. You will sirnply spend some
time as a guest at the lodging, photograph the surroundingrs, and
submit your favorable or unfavorable observations to the editor of
a travel guide. Therefore, the value of the accommodations is a
gift to you fron the provider of the accommodations.
cifts are lirnited under the provisions of the Act as follows:
(a) No member of a state board or commission,
and no designated ernployee of a state agency, shall
accept gifts with a total value of more than two
hundred fifty dollars (9250) in a cal-endar year
from any single source, if the uenber or employee
hrould be required to report the receipt of income
or gifts from that source on his or her statement
of economic interest. 
This paragraph shal1 not
apply to any part-time member of the governing
board of any pubtic institution of higher
education, unless the member is also an elected
official.
Section 89505 (a) .
Therefore, you may not receive gifts in excess of 5250 from any
one source.
For purposes of the Act, gifts constitute income- (Section
82030(a),) Therefore, the fair narket value of the courtesy or
discounted acconnodations is reportable as income on Commission
Form 73o Statenents of Economic Interests if valued at $50 or
more. (Section 872o7(a) (1) and Regul,ation L8726, copy enclosed.)
we trust this Letter adequately responds to your i,nquiry.
Shoul,d you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call ne at (916) 322-5901-
S incere 1y ,
Scott Hall,abrin
Acting cenera] counsel

EncloSures
counsel,
t egal Division
